title: The mesoscopic foundations of non equilibrium thermodynamics and the arrow of time in the Dual Model of Liquids
abstract: This manuscripts has two goals. The first goal is to show that the interaction in the Dual Model of Liquids between the solidlike molecule aggregates and the lattice excitations is appropriate to represent the link between the behaviour at macroscopic scale of normal liquids and the physical processes characterizing those systems at mesoscopic scale. The second goal is to show that the duality allows identifying a time arrow on the mesoscopic scale in liquids. The interaction of quanta of elastic energy with the molecular clusters introduces a privileged direction, which is relevant in time dependent and dissipative macroscopic processes, although the interaction remains temporally reversible
summary: This paper develops the Dual Model of Liquids (DML) to bridge macroscopic non-equilibrium thermodynamics with mesoscopic physics in liquids. It models energy transport via wave-packet interactions between solid-like aggregates and lattice excitations. The key finding is that this duality introduces a necessary asymmetry at the mesoscopic scale, providing a physical basis for the arrow of time.
main_problem: To establish a physical link between macroscopic non-equilibrium thermodynamics (NET) and mesoscopic microscopic processes in liquids, while simultaneously identifying the origin of the arrow of time.
main_result: The Dual Model of Liquids successfully models transport coefficients and suggests that the inherent duality introduces a mesoscopic asymmetry responsible for the thermodynamic arrow of time, even though elementary interactions are reversible.
method: Theoretical modeling using the Dual Model of Liquids (DML), which treats energy/momentum exchange between solid-like aggregates ('icebergs') and lattice excitations via quasi-elastic wave-packet dynamics.
model_or_system: The system is liquid matter, modeled by the DML. This model posits that liquids exhibit dualism, sharing energy transmission modes with solids through collective excitations (wave-packets) interacting with metastable solid-like clusters.
key_observables: Macroscopic transport properties like thermal conductivity, viscosity, and Soret coefficient; mesoscopic observables include propagating transverse currents and relaxation times ($	au$).
important_parameters: Mesoscopic scale (intermediate between atomic quantum mechanics and fluid dynamics); characteristic time scales ($	au$); energy/momentum exchange parameters ($\Delta E$, $\Delta p$).
main_assumptions: The DML assumes that solid-like aggregates maintain a quasi-harmonic structure over relaxation timescales, allowing wave propagation; NET is treated as phenomenological.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the elementary energy/momentum exchange between lattice and liquid particles (collision dynamics), the mechanism for viscosity via wave-packets mediating layer friction, and the comparison of time evolution under equilibrium versus irreversible processes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need to bridge NET with microscopic physics. It then details the DML framework, analyzing energy/momentum exchange mechanisms (collisions, tunneling) that yield macroscopic transport laws, culminating in an explanation for thermodynamic irreversibility at the mesoscopic level.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, physically motivated mechanism—wave-packet interaction and structural relaxation—to derive irreversible thermodynamics from time-reversible microscopic dynamics, which is central to open quantum systems theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 1. Schematic representation of inelastic collisions between wave-packets and liquid particles. This is the elementary interaction which the DML is based on.
Figure 2. Frequency spectrum of the elastic perturbations propagating through media, with indication of the phononic spectrum.

Figure 3. The pictorial model of liquids at mesoscopic scale, on which the DML is based: icebergs of solid lattice fluctuating and interacting within the liquid global system at equilibrium. It reflects also what may be deduced from experiments performed with IXS techniques [67-88], able to observe liquids at such scale-lengths.
